current_conditions.site_name: SOQUE RIVER AT GA 197, NEAR CLARKESVILLE, GA

light_pressure_read.read: High pressure and low cloud cover make this fishable but technical.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

caution_notes: Bright/high-pressure conditions may make fish cover-oriented and less forgiving.

generated_text.quick_read: The Soque River currently offers fair trout fishing conditions with a flow of 65.1 cfs and a gage height of 3.56 ft. Water temperature data is unavailable, but recent stocking enhances fishing prospects.
generated_text.best_window: Day 1 morning is best for trout fishing due to low wind speeds and moderate sky cover, supporting more comfortable trout activity near shade and structure despite high ambient pressure making fish somewhat technical.
generated_text.current_conditions: USGS reports a flow of 65.1 cfs and gage height of 3.56 ft; water temperature and turbidity are unavailable.
generated_text.weather_setup: Current weather shows mostly clear skies with 20.91% average sky cover, high pressure at 30.11 inHg with no recent trend data, light east winds averaging 2.2 mph, and only a 14% chance of minimal rain.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will favor shaded, deeper runs and undercut banks, especially near structure, moving cautiously due to bright high pressure. Use smaller natural flies and careful drifts with less aggressive presentations; recommended flies include egg patterns, squirmy worms, duracell attractors, zebra midges, and pheasant tails. Expect fish to seek cover and be less forgiving under these conditions.
